messages.content: How to clear input from linux terminal

You can clear the input from the Linux terminal by using the following command:

`Ctrl + U`

This will clear the current line you are typing on.
How to install golang in linux cli

You can install Golang in Linux CLI by following these simple steps:

1. Open the terminal on your Linux system.

2. Add the Golang PPA repository to your system with the following command:

   ```
   sudo add-apt-repository ppa:longsleep/golang-backports
   ```

3. Update the package list with the command:

   ```
   sudo apt-get update
   ```

4. Install Golang using the command:

   ```
   sudo apt-get install golang-go
   ```

5. Verify the installation by running the following command:

   ```
   go version
   ```

   This will display the installed version of Golang on your system.

That's it. Golang is now installed on your Linux system and ready to use.
Now how do i uninstall it

To uninstall or remove Golang from your Linux system, you can use the following command:

```
sudo apt-get remove golang-go
```

This command will remove the Golang package and any associated files from your system.

If you want to completely remove Golang and all its dependencies, you can use the following command instead:

```
sudo apt-get autoremove golang-go
```

This command will remove Golang and any dependencies that are no longer needed by other packages.

After running either of these commands, you can verify that Golang has been removed by checking the version using the `go version` command. It should return an error message indicating that the `go` command is not found.
how to install snap on debian

To install Snap on Debian, you can follow these steps:

1. Open the terminal on your Debian system.

2. Update the package list using the following command:
   
   ```
   sudo apt update   
   ```

3. Install the Snapd package using the following command:

   ```
   sudo apt install snapd   
   ```

4. After installing Snapd, the Snap service should start automatically. If it doesn't, you can start it manually with the following command:

   ```
   sudo systemctl start snapd.service   
   ```
   
5. Verify that the Snap service is running with the following command:

   ```
   sudo systemctl status snapd.service   
   ```

6. Finally, you can install any Snap package by running the following command:

   ```
   sudo snap install [package-name]   
   ```

That’s it. You have successfully installed Snap on Debian and can now use it to install and manage your favorite Snap packages.
How do i exit the systemctl after running that command

You can exit the `systemctl` command by pressing `q` or `Ctrl + C` keys on your keyboard. 

`q` key immediately exits from the command output and brings you back to the terminal prompt, whereas `Ctrl + C` sends an interrupt signal to the command and terminates it. 

After exiting, you can continue using your terminal for other tasks.
After installing snap i did goversion and it

Installing Snap should not affect the `go version` command as they are unrelated. If you are getting an error like "command not found" when running the `go version` command, it may be because the Golang binary is not in your system's `$PATH`.

You can check if Golang is installed on your system and get its path by running the following command in your terminal:

```
which go
```

If Golang is installed, it should show its path such as `/usr/local/go/bin/go`.

If it shows a message saying that the command is not found, then Golang may not be installed on your system.

In that case, you can install Golang using the appropriate method for your system. You can refer to the previous answer on how to install Golang on Linux CLI.
how to uninstall go with snap

If you installed Golang using Snap, you can uninstall it using the following command:

```
sudo snap remove go
```

This command will remove the Golang Snap package from your system.

After running this command, you can verify that Golang has been removed by checking the version using the `go version` command. It should return an error message indicating that the `go` command is not found.
how to install this file in debian: go1.20.5.linux-amd64.tar.gz

To install Golang from a tarball on Debian, you can follow these steps:

1. Download the Golang tarball from the official Golang website using the following command:

   ```
   wget https://golang.org/dl/go1.20.5.linux-amd64.tar.gz
   ```

   This will download the Golang tarball to your current working directory.

2. Extract the Golang tarball using the following command:

   ```
   tar -C /usr/local -xzf go1.20.5.linux-amd64.tar.gz
   ```

   This command will extract the Golang tarball into the `/usr/local` directory.

3. Add the Golang binary directory to your system's `$PATH` variable by appending the following lines to your `~/.profile` file, using your preferred text editor:

   ```
   export PATH=$PATH:/usr/local/go/bin
   ```

   This will add Golang's `/usr/local/go/bin` directory to your system's `$PATH` variable, allowing you to use the `go` command from any directory.

4. Reload your `~/.profile` file to apply the changes by running the following command:

   ```
   source ~/.profile
   ```

5. Verify that Golang is installed and working by running the following command to check the version:

   ```
   go version
   ```

   It should display the installed version of Golang on your system.

That's it! You have now successfully installed Golang from a tarball on Debian.

Sure, here's step 3 again with some more explanation:

3. Add the Golang binary directory to your system's `$PATH` variable by appending the following lines to your `~/.profile` file:

   ```
   export PATH=$PATH:/usr/local/go/bin
   ```

   Here, the `export PATH=$PATH:` part tells the system to append the following path to the existing `$PATH` variable, and `/usr/local/go/bin` is the directory where the Golang binary is installed.

   The `~/.profile` file is a shell script that is run by your shell when you log in. By adding this line to your `~/.profile` file, you're telling your shell to add the Golang binary directory to your `$PATH` variable every time you log in to your system.

   You can open the `~/.profile` file using your preferred text editor. For example, you can use the `nano` editor by running the following command:

   ```
   nano ~/.profile
   ```

   Then, append the `export` line to the end of the file, save it, and exit the editor.

   After making changes to your `~/.profile` file, you need to reload it into your current shell session using the `source` command, as mentioned in step 4. This will apply the changes immediately.
